title: The Rule
narrative: An abbot presents you with a schedule: vigils at midnight, prostrations at dawn, one meal, no conversation after Compline. He does not ask if you agree. He simply hands you the timetable.

choices.text: Accept the rule without question
Ask why the rule is structured this way
Offer to take an additional fast

title: The Cell
narrative: The elder's direction: stay in your cell. You are not confined — the door is unlocked. But everything the world offers is outside it, and everything the soul requires is inside. After three days, you begin to understand that the cell is not a room. It is a practice.

choices.text: Stay and meet what the cell brings
Read until you stop running from silence
Pray the hours without leaving

title: The Novice Master
narrative: He corrects you seventeen times in a single morning. Not harshly — with patience, which is harder to receive. Each correction is accurate. By the seventeenth you feel the choice: receive it as teaching or receive it as attack. The monk watches to see which you choose.

choices.text: Bow and say thank you each time
Ask what he sees that you cannot
Correct what he named without speaking

title: The Chapter of Faults
narrative: Once a week, the community gathers. Any monk may confess a fault before the others. An older monk speaks: 'I judged a brother in my heart and called it discernment.' The room is completely still. No one corrects him. No one praises him. The silence is the absolution.

choices.text: Confess what you have been holding
Receive the silence as your own
Pray for the monk who spoke

title: The Kitchen
narrative: For three months, obedience assigns you to the kitchen. Onions and soup and dishwater. The abbot who runs the kitchen is known as the most advanced hesychast in the community. He has not been given the chance to pray in silence for thirty years. He is never not praying.

choices.text: Chop the onions with full attention
Ask him to teach you while you work
Serve the evening meal with care

title: The Sick Brother
narrative: He has been ill for six months. The monastery rule permits reduced vigils for the sick, but he insists on attending all of them. The infirmarian has asked the abbot to forbid it. The abbot has not yet decided. He asks your opinion, which surprises you.

choices.text: Say the love that drives him is worth the cost
Say the body is also a gift to protect
Say you do not know and bow

title: The Midnight Canon
narrative: You have been awake for nineteen hours. The canon is long. The reader's voice fills the dark chapel and the words are entering you not through understanding but through repetition, the way a stream enters stone. You are not sure you are awake. Something in you is.

choices.text: Stay standing until the last amen
Let the words carry you without understanding
Offer the weariness as the prayer

title: The Departure
narrative: A monk has decided to leave. He has been here twelve years. He says nothing is wrong; he simply feels called elsewhere. The community does not argue. The elder blesses him at the gate, and adds: 'I hope you find what you are looking for. But I suspect it is here.'

choices.text: Walk with him to the gate in silence
Ask him what called him elsewhere
Return to your cell and examine your own callings

title: Lectio Divina
narrative: The text is read once quickly, then slowly, then once more in silence. Not study — encounter. The fathers distinguished between theology as information and theology as presence. The same verse that ran through you yesterday lands differently today. You are not the same reader.

choices.text: Read until the text reads you
Memorize the verse that landed
Sit in silence after the third reading

title: The Elder's Silence
narrative: You asked him a question two weeks ago. He has not answered. He has not forgotten — you have seen him pause twice when you passed, as if gathering something. Today he calls you in and says four words. They answer the question, and three others you have not yet thought to ask.

choices.text: Receive the four words without adding more
Ask him what the other three questions are
Bow and go practice the answer
